senses.definition: A chemical compound (a natural product) identified in scientific and medical research, not used in everyday language.
examples: Researchers detected athamantin in the plant extract during the laboratory analysis.
The study listed athamantin among several bioactive compounds found in the sample.

senses.definition: An archaic spelling of “adamant”, meaning a very hard substance or, figuratively, something unbreakable.
examples: The knight’s shield was said to be made of athamaunte and could not be pierced.
In the old tale, her resolve was athamaunte and never shaken.

senses.definition: A ceremonial knife, usually with a black handle, used in Wiccan or other neo‑pagan rituals to direct energy rather than to cut physical objects.
examples: The witch placed her athame on the altar before beginning the ritual.
In many Wiccan traditions, the athame is used to cast the circle.
He bought a new athame with a carved wooden handle for his ceremonies.

senses.definition: The plural of athame: ceremonial knives used in Wiccan or other neo‑pagan rituals.
examples: Different covens may design their athames in unique styles.
The shop sells robes, candles, and athames for modern pagans.

senses.definition: The state of never dying; immortality.
examples: Many myths explore the idea of athanasia and eternal youth.
The hero was granted athanasia as a reward from the gods.
Some science‑fiction stories imagine technology that gives humans athanasia.

senses.definition: Relating to Athanasius, a fourth‑century Christian bishop, or to the Christian doctrines associated with him, especially about the Trinity.
examples: The council affirmed an Athanasian understanding of the Trinity.
Her research focuses on Athanasian theology in the early church.

senses.definition: A follower of the teachings of Athanasius or a believer in the Athanasian Creed.
examples: The Athanasians strongly opposed the Arian movement.
As an Athanasian, he defended the traditional doctrine of the Trinity.

senses.definition: The Christian doctrines expressed in or derived from the Athanasian Creed, especially the belief in the Trinity and the full divinity of Christ.
examples: Athanasianism became the official position of many Western churches.
The article compares Athanasianism with various forms of Arian belief.
Critics argued that Athanasianism was too complex for ordinary believers.

senses.definition: A person who supports or follows the doctrines of the Athanasian Creed.
examples: The Athanasianists argued fiercely against their Arian opponents.
As an Athanasianist, he emphasized the equality of the three divine Persons.

senses.definition: Plural of Athanasy: personal name or related forms, mainly appearing in historical or onomastic contexts.
examples: The register lists several Athanasies born in the same village.
Family records show that the Athanasies were related through marriage.

senses.definition: A rare variant of “athanasia”, meaning immortality or the state of not dying.
examples: The philosopher wrote about athanasy as the ultimate human dream.
Legends of athanasy often end with unexpected tragedy.

senses.definition: A furnace used by alchemists that keeps a steady heat for a long time.
examples: The alchemist tended the athanor through the night to maintain the slow fire.
In the old laboratory, an unused athanor stood against the wall.
The recipe required the mixture to stay in the athanor for forty days.

senses.definition: The plural of athanor: furnaces used by alchemists to keep a constant heat.
examples: The museum displays several athanors from different centuries.
Old texts describe how to build athanors for various experiments.

senses.definition: A member of any Indigenous group in North America that speaks an Athapaskan (Athabaskan) language.
examples: Many Athapascans traditionally lived in the subarctic regions of Canada and Alaska.
The Athapascan hunted caribou and fished along the rivers.

senses.definition: Relating to Athapascan peoples, their languages, or their culture.
examples: She is studying Athapascan languages at university.
The exhibition features Athapascan art and tools.

senses.definition: A member of any Indigenous group in North America that speaks an Athapaskan (Athabaskan) language.
examples: Some Athapaskans later moved south and became the ancestors of the Apache and Navajo.
Today, many Athapaskans work to preserve their traditional languages.

senses.definition: Relating to Athapaskan peoples, their languages, or their culture.
examples: She is a specialist in Athapaskan linguistics.
Athapaskan oral traditions are rich in stories and songs.

senses.definition: A male given name of Arabic origin, used in some Muslim communities.
examples: Athar presented his research at the conference.
Dr. Athar has practiced medicine for more than twenty years.

senses.definition: Relating to the Atharva Veda, the fourth Veda in Hindu tradition, or to its priests.
examples: The Atharvan texts include many spells and charms.
Atharvan rituals often deal with healing and protection.

senses.definition: A priest associated with the Atharva Veda in ancient Indian religion.
examples: The king consulted an Atharvan before the ceremony.
Atharvans were responsible for certain household rituals.

senses.definition: A simple substitution cipher of the Hebrew alphabet in which the first letter is replaced by the last, the second by the second‑to‑last, and so on.
examples: The scholar explained how the word was encoded using the Athbash cipher.
In some interpretations, certain biblical names are seen as Athbash codes.

senses.definition: A biological term describing groups of animals or plants that lack a case or sheath, used mainly in older scientific texts.
examples: The species was formerly placed among the Athecae.
Older zoological works divide the group into Thecatae and Athecae.

senses.definition: A taxonomic group of hydroids that lack a protective cup around the polyps, used in zoology.
examples: The jellyfish belongs to the order Athecata.
Athecata were once separated from Thecata based on the presence of a sheath.

senses.definition: Without a protective case, cup, or sheath, especially in descriptions of certain hydroids and related organisms.
examples: The colony has athecate polyps exposed to the water.
Athecate forms lack the small cup that covers the hydranths.

senses.definition: A British spelling of “atheize”: to make someone or something atheistic, or to interpret something in an atheistic way, used rarely and often historically.
examples: The regime tried to atheise public life by closing the churches.
Some critics feared that science would atheise society.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of “atheise”: made atheistic or treated in an atheistic way.
examples: Public institutions were largely atheised during that period.
He argued that modern culture had been atheised by materialism.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of “atheise”: makes atheistic or interprets in an atheistic way.
examples: He claims that the new philosophy atheises all traditional values.
The policy effectively atheises public education.

senses.definition: Present participle of “atheise”: making atheistic or treating in an atheistic way.
examples: Critics accused the government of atheising the school system.
By removing religious symbols, they were gradually atheising public space.

senses.definition: The belief that there is no god or gods, or the state of not believing in any god or gods.
examples: He turned to atheism after years of questioning his faith.
Atheism is discussed alongside other worldviews in the course.
Some people see atheism as simply a lack of belief in deities.

senses.definition: Plural of atheism: different forms or expressions of not believing in any god or gods.
examples: The book compares various atheisms found in modern culture.
Different atheisms offer different reasons for rejecting belief in gods.

senses.definition: A person who does not believe in the existence of any god or gods.
examples: She is an atheist but respects other people’s religious beliefs.
As an atheist, he relies on science rather than scripture.
Many atheists still celebrate traditional holidays for cultural reasons.

senses.definition: Relating to or characteristic of atheists or atheism.
examples: The author presents an atheist perspective on morality.
Some countries have major atheist organizations.

senses.definition: Holding or showing the belief that there is no god or gods; related to atheism.
examples: He grew up in an atheistic household.
The book promotes an atheistic worldview.
Some people see certain political systems as atheistic.

senses.definition: Old‑fashioned form of atheistic: showing or related to the belief that there is no god or gods, often used disapprovingly.
examples: His opponents attacked him for his atheistical opinions.
The pamphlet condemned what it called atheistical teachings in the schools.

senses.definition: In a way that shows or supports the belief that there is no god or gods.
examples: The author writes atheistically about questions of meaning and purpose.
He spoke atheistically, denying any role for divine beings.

senses.definition: The quality of being atheistical; the state of rejecting belief in any god or gods, often used in an old‑fashioned or critical way.
examples: They complained about the atheisticalness of modern culture.
In that era, atheisticalness was seen as a serious moral problem.

senses.definition: The quality of being atheistic; the condition of not believing in any god or gods.
examples: Her atheisticness did not stop her from appreciating religious art.
The article discusses the increasing atheisticness of younger generations.

senses.definition: Plural of atheist: people who do not believe in the existence of any god or gods.
examples: Atheists and believers took part in the discussion.
Many atheists support the separation of church and state.

senses.definition: To make someone or something atheistic, or to treat or explain something in an atheistic way; rare and mainly historical.
examples: Some feared that modern philosophy would atheize the nation.
Revolutionary leaders tried to atheize public life.
He argued that science does not necessarily atheize our view of the world.

senses.definition: Past tense and past participle of “atheize”: made atheistic or interpreted in an atheistic way.
examples: The state became increasingly atheized during the twentieth century.
Some regions were more thoroughly atheized than others.

senses.definition: A person who causes others or society to become atheistic, or who explains things in an atheistic way; rare and often disapproving.
examples: He was denounced as an atheizer of the young.
The critics portrayed the philosopher as a dangerous atheizer.

senses.definition: Third person singular present of “atheize”: makes atheistic or interprets in an atheistic way.
examples: He claims that modern science atheizes traditional beliefs.
The policy effectively atheizes the public sphere.

senses.definition: Present participle of “atheize”: making atheistic or treating in an atheistic way.
examples: Critics accused the movement of atheizing the nation’s culture.
By removing religious instruction, they were gradually atheizing the schools.

senses.definition: A name for certain trees or shrubs of the tamarisk family, especially those known as athel trees in desert regions.
examples: The athel provides shade in the otherwise barren landscape.
Farmers planted rows of athel as a windbreak.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which a person is born without one or both nipples.
examples: Athelia may occur on its own or as part of a wider syndrome.
The doctor explained that athelia is rare but can be treated cosmetically.

senses.definition: In early English history, a prince of the royal family, especially one who had the right to become king.
examples: The ætheling was raised at court to prepare for possible rule.
Several æthelings competed for the throne after the king’s death.
The chronicle mentions an atheling who died in battle.

senses.definition: Plural of atheling: princes of the royal family in early English history who could inherit the throne.
examples: The king’s sons were all considered athelings.
The council chose one of the athelings to succeed the king.

senses.definition: In grammar, describing a verb that forms its present tense without using a regular thematic vowel, especially in ancient Indo‑European languages.
examples: In this language, a few high‑frequency verbs are athematic.
Students often struggle with the irregular endings of athematic verbs.

senses.definition: In an athematic way; according to the patterns of athematic verbs in historical linguistics.
examples: The verb is conjugated athematically in the present tense.
These forms behave athematically and must be memorized.

senses.definition: In Greek mythology, the goddess of wisdom, war, and crafts, often shown with a helmet and a shield.
examples: Athena was worshipped as the protector of the city of Athens.
In the myth, Athena springs fully grown from Zeus’s head.
The temple on the hill was dedicated to Athena.

senses.definition: A female given name, taken from the Greek goddess Athena.
examples: Athena moved to London when she was a child.
They named their daughter Athena after the Greek goddess.

senses.definition: Plural of athenaeum: literary or scientific clubs, libraries, or buildings used for such societies, mainly in historical or formal contexts.
examples: Nineteenth‑century cities often founded athenaea for self‑education.
The report lists several athenaea established across the country.

senses.definition: A building or club used for literary, artistic, or scientific meetings, reading, or study.
examples: He spent many evenings reading at the local athenaeum.
The city’s athenaeum hosts lectures on literature and history.
Members of the athenaeum can borrow books from its library.

senses.definition: Plural of athenaeum: buildings or clubs for literary, artistic, or scientific activities.
examples: Several athenaeums were founded in the nineteenth century.
The country’s athenaeums played an important role in adult education.

senses.definition: A mainly French term for a secondary school or cultural institution; in English it appears chiefly in names of particular institutions.
examples: He studied at the Athénée in Brussels.
The Athenee offers evening classes for adults.

senses.definition: Variant spelling of athenaeum: a building or club used for literary, artistic, or scientific meetings, reading, or study.
examples: The town’s Atheneum was founded by local businessmen.
Lectures at the Atheneum are open to the public.
